critique.summary: The Free Software Foundation (FSF) discusses a class action lawsuit, Bartz v. Anthropic, alleging copyright infringement by Anthropic for using copyrighted works in training large language models (LLMs). The FSF emphasizes the importance of software freedom and urges LLM developers to share training data, models, and source code freely.
mainPoints: Bartz v. Anthropic is a class action lawsuit claiming Anthropic infringed copyright by downloading books from Library Genesis and Pirate Library Mirror datasets for LLM training.
A district court ruled that using the books to train LLMs was fair use, but legality of downloading the datasets remains unresolved.
The parties agreed to settle before trial, offering potential copyright holders money instead of damages.
FSF holds copyrights to GNU Project programs and books, including 'Free as in freedom' by Sam Williams and Richard Stallman, which was found in Anthropic's training datasets.
FSF advocates for sharing complete training inputs, models, configurations, and source code to protect computing freedom.
FSF states it would seek user freedom as compensation if its copyrights were violated in such lawsuits.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately reports on the existence of the lawsuit Bartz v. Anthropic and the general legal issues surrounding the use of copyrighted materials in training LLMs, including the fair use ruling on training use. The FSF's copyrights on GNU Project materials and their advocacy for software freedom are well-known and verifiable. However, the article does not cite multiple independent sources or perspectives on the lawsuit or settlement, and the details about the settlement offer and FSF's potential legal actions appear to come solely from FSF statements. The article's claims about the court ruling and settlement align with known legal principles but lack external corroboration.
considerations: The article does not provide perspectives from Anthropic or other defendants in the lawsuit.
There is no independent verification of the settlement terms or the scope of the datasets used.
The legal distinction between downloading datasets and using them for training is complex and evolving, which the article only briefly addresses.
The FSF's position reflects its advocacy for free software and may not represent all copyright holders or legal experts.
The article does not discuss potential impacts on AI development or broader copyright law implications.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the FSF's stance regarding the Bartz v. Anthropic lawsuit and its implications for copyright and open licensing of AI models. Commenters debate whether the FSF is threatening legal action or merely stating a principled position, the accuracy of the Hacker News title, and the broader issues of copyright enforcement and AI training data transparency.

comments.author: mjg59
comments.text:
Where's the threat? The FSF was notified that as part of the settlement in Bartz v. Anthropic they were potentially entitled to money, but in this case the works in question were released under a license that allowed free duplication and distribution so no harm was caused. There's then a note that if the FSF had been involved in such a suit they'd insist on any settlement requiring that the trained model be released under a free license. But they weren't, and they're not.

(Edit: In the event of it being changed to match the actual article title, the current subject line for this thread is " FSF Threatens Anthropic over Infringed Copyright: Share Your LLMs Freel")
comments.analysis: This comment provides a clear, fact-based summary of the FSF's position and the nature of the works involved, accurately reflecting the situation without speculation.

comments.author: grodriguez100
comments.text:
Is the FSF threatening Anthropic? The way I read it looks like they are not:

> We are a small organization with limited resources and we have to pick our battles, but if the FSF were to participate in a lawsuit such as Bartz v. Anthropic and find our copyright and license violated, we would certainly request user freedom as compensation.

Sounds more like “we can’t and won’t sue, but this is the kind of compensation that we think would be appropriate”
comments.analysis: This comment correctly interprets the FSF's statement as a conditional stance rather than an active threat, clarifying the organization's limited resources.

comments.author: bobokaytop
comments.text: The framing of 'share your weights freely' as a remedy is interesting but underspecified. The FSF's argument is essentially that training on copyrighted code without permission is infringement, and the remedy should be open weights. But open weights don't undo the infringement -- they just make a potentially infringing artifact publicly available. That's not how copyright remedies work. What they're actually asking for is more like a compulsory license, which Congress would have to create. The demand for open weights as a copyright remedy is a policy argument dressed up as a legal one.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully critiques the FSF's proposed remedy, distinguishing between legal copyright remedies and policy advocacy.

comments.author: rvz
comments.text:
> Among the works we hold copyrights over is Sam Williams and Richard Stallman's Free as in freedom: Richard Stallman's crusade for free software, which was found in datasets used by Anthropic as training inputs for their LLMs.

This is the reason why AI companies won't let anyone inspect which content was in the training set. It turns out the suspicions from many copyright holders (including the FSF) was true (of course).

Anthropic and others will never admit it, hence why they wanted to settle and not risk going to trial. AI boosters obviously will continue to gaslight copyright holders to believe nonsense like: "It only scraped the links, so AI didn't directly train on your content!", or "AI can't see like humans, it only see numbers, binary or digits" or "AI didn't reproduce exactly 100% of the content just like humans do when tracing from memory!".

They will not share the data-set used to train Claude, even if it was trained on AGPLv3 code.
comments.analysis: While somewhat speculative about company motives, this comment highlights the transparency issues and legal risks around AI training data, reflecting common industry concerns.

comments.author: kavalg
comments.text:
It looks like the stance of FSF is for proliferation of the copyleft to trained LLMs

> "Therefore, we urge Anthropic and other LLM developers that train models using huge datasets downloaded from the Internet to provide these LLMs to their users in freedom"
comments.analysis: This comment accurately summarizes the FSF's ideological stance on copyleft licensing for AI models.

comments.author: jamesnorden
comments.text: The FSF seems toothless when it comes to actually enforcing anything regarding license violations.
comments.analysis: Though somewhat subjective, this comment reflects a common criticism of the FSF's limited enforcement capabilities.

comments.author: latexr
comments.text: What weak, counter-productive, messaging. This is like having a bully punching you in the face and responding with “hey man, I’m not going to do anything about this, I’m not even going to tell an adult, but I’d urge you to consider not punching me in the face”. Great news for the bully! You just removed one concern from their mind, essentially giving the permission to be as bad to you as they want.
comments.analysis: This comment uses a strong metaphor but lacks nuance and constructive critique, reducing its analytical value.

comments.author: raincole
comments.text: HN really needs some stricter rules for editorialized title. The HN title has nothing to do with the link (unless the article is edited?)
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid point about misleading headlines but does not engage deeply with the substance of the FSF statement.

comments.author: charcircuit
comments.text:
>share complete training inputs with every user of the LLM

They don't have the rights to distribute the training data.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly points out legal limitations on distributing copyrighted training data, adding important context.

comments.author: slopinthebag
comments.text: Good. I want to see more lawsuits going after these hyper scalers for blatantly disregarding copyright law while simultaneously benefiting from it. In a just world they would all go down and we would be left with just the OSS models. But we don't live in a fair world :(
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a personal viewpoint favoring enforcement but lacks detailed legal or technical analysis.
